expanded_description: A comprehensive examination of muscle tissue—the contractile tissue responsible for movement, posture, and visceral function—detailing the three muscle types: skeletal muscle (voluntary, striated, multinucleated, attached to skeleton), cardiac muscle (involuntary, striated, branched, intercalated discs, heart only), and smooth muscle (involuntary, non-striated, spindle-shaped, walls of hollow organs), with emphasis on structural characteristics, functional properties, regulatory mechanisms, and clinical correlations.

core_concept.principle: The three muscle tissue types—skeletal, cardiac, and smooth—share the fundamental property of contractility (actin-myosin interaction) but differ in location, structure, regulation (voluntary vs. involuntary), striation pattern, cellular organization, and functional characteristics (speed of contraction, fatigue resistance, rhythmicity), reflecting their specialized roles in locomotion, circulation, and visceral function.
core_concept.explanation: Muscle tissue is specialized for contraction, generating force and movement. Three types based on structure, function, and regulation: (1) Skeletal muscle—attached to bones; voluntary control; long cylindrical, multinucleated fibers; striated (alternating A/I bands); rapid, powerful contractions; fatigable; innervated by somatic motor neurons. (2) Cardiac muscle—heart only; involuntary; branched cells; single centrally located nucleus; intercalated discs (gap junctions, desmosomes); striated; autorhythmic; sustained, fatigue-resistant; innervated by autonomic nervous system (modulates rate). (3) Smooth muscle—walls of hollow organs (blood vessels, GI tract, uterus, bladder); involuntary; spindle-shaped, single central nucleus; non-striated; slow, sustained contractions; fatigue-resistant; innervated by autonomic nervous system; responds to hormones, local factors. Common features: contractile proteins (actin, myosin) arranged in sarcomeres (skeletal, cardiac) or less organized (smooth); excitation-contraction coupling (depolarization → Ca²⁺ increase → contraction); energy utilization (ATP hydrolysis). Clinical correlations: muscular dystrophy (skeletal muscle degeneration), myocardial infarction (cardiac muscle death), smooth muscle disorders (asthma—bronchospasm, hypertension—vascular smooth muscle, achalasia—esophageal dysmotility). Understanding muscle types is fundamental for physiology, pathology, pharmacology, and clinical medicine.

image.alt: Microscopic comparison of skeletal (striated, multinucleated), cardiac (striated, branched, intercalated discs), and smooth (non-striated, spindle-shaped) muscle
image.caption: Muscle tissue types: Skeletal (voluntary, striated, multinucleated), Cardiac (involuntary, striated, branched, intercalated discs), Smooth (involuntary, non-striated, spindle-shaped). All share actin-myosin contraction but differ in structure, regulation, function.

sections.section_title: Introduction: Muscle Tissue as the Motor of the Body
sections.introduction: Muscle tissue constitutes approximately 40-50% of total body mass, serving as the body's motor—generating force for locomotion, maintaining posture, propelling blood, moving food through the digestive tract, and regulating flow through hollow organs. The three muscle types—skeletal, cardiac, and smooth—represent a remarkable evolutionary diversification of the basic contractile mechanism (actin-myosin sliding filament theory) to meet diverse functional demands. Skeletal muscle: striated, voluntary, attached to skeleton via tendons; enables precise, rapid movements from fine finger control to powerful leg extension; fatigue occurs with sustained activity. Cardiac muscle: striated, involuntary, exclusive to heart; cells connected by intercalated discs (gap junctions for electrical coupling, desmosomes for mechanical strength); autorhythmic (pacemaker cells generate intrinsic rhythm); contracts 60-100 times/minute for a lifetime without fatigue. Smooth muscle: non-striated, involuntary, widely distributed in walls of hollow organs; slow, sustained contractions; maintains tone (vascular resistance, sphincters); responds to autonomic nerves, hormones, local metabolites. Despite their differences, all muscle types share fundamental properties: excitability (respond to stimuli), contractility (generate force), extensibility (stretch without damage), elasticity (return to resting length). Understanding muscle tissue types is essential for comprehending movement, circulation, digestion, and numerous clinical disorders—from muscular dystrophy to heart failure to asthma.

introduction_image.alt: Human body showing skeletal muscles (locomotion), heart (cardiac muscle), and visceral organs (smooth muscle)
introduction_image.caption: Muscle tissue distribution: Skeletal (locomotion, posture—40-50% body mass), Cardiac (heart—pump blood), Smooth (hollow organs—visceral function). Each type specialized for functional demands.

key_concept.definition: The three muscle tissue types share the contractile machinery (actin, myosin, sliding filament mechanism) but differ in location, cellular structure, striation, regulation (voluntary vs. involuntary), contraction speed, fatigue resistance, and neural control—specializations reflecting their distinct functional roles.
key_concept.context: Skeletal muscle: voluntary, rapid, powerful, fatigable—optimized for locomotion, manipulation. Cardiac muscle: involuntary, autorhythmic, fatigue-resistant—optimized for continuous rhythmic pumping. Smooth muscle: involuntary, slow, sustained, metabolically efficient—optimized for maintaining tone, propelling contents through hollow organs. Clinical relevance: muscle type-specific diseases—skeletal (muscular dystrophy, myasthenia gravis), cardiac (cardiomyopathy, heart failure), smooth (asthma, hypertension, achalasia). Pharmacologic targeting differs: neuromuscular blockers affect skeletal; β-blockers, calcium channel blockers affect cardiac; antispasmodics affect smooth.

image.alt: Comparative table: skeletal, cardiac, smooth muscle—location, striation, nuclei, control, speed, fatigue
image.caption: Muscle type comparison: Skeletal (voluntary, striated, multinucleated, rapid, fatigable); Cardiac (involuntary, striated, mononucleated, intercalated discs, fatigue-resistant); Smooth (involuntary, non-striated, mononucleated, slow, sustained).

sections.key_concept: Skeletal muscle is voluntary, striated, and multinucleated; cardiac muscle is involuntary, striated, and branched with intercalated discs; smooth muscle is involuntary, non-striated, and spindle-shaped—each with unique structural and functional specializations.

classifications.value: Best for understanding voluntary movement, force generation, and neuromuscular transmission
characteristics: Location: Attached to bones via tendons (origin, insertion)
Cell shape: Long cylindrical fibers (up to 30 cm)
Nuclei: Multiple, peripherally located (multinucleated)
Striation: Present (alternating A/I bands, Z-discs)
Control: Voluntary (somatic motor neurons)
Contraction speed: Fast (30-100 msec to peak tension)
Fatigue: Fatigable (depends on fiber type)
Regeneration: Limited (satellite cells provide some regenerative capacity)
classifications.behavior: Skeletal muscle is optimized for rapid, powerful, voluntary movement. Muscle fibers are multinucleated because they form from fusion of myoblasts during development; nuclei remain peripheral, leaving central space for contractile elements. Striations result from precise arrangement of thick (myosin) and thin (actin) filaments into sarcomeres—the functional contractile units. Innervation: each muscle fiber receives one neuromuscular junction (end plate); motor unit = one motor neuron + all muscle fibers it innervates (ranges from 5-10 for fine control to 1,000+ for powerful muscles). Excitation-contraction coupling: action potential travels along sarcolemma, down T-tubules → triggers Ca²⁺ release from sarcoplasmic reticulum (ryanodine receptors) → Ca²⁺ binds troponin → tropomyosin moves → cross-bridge cycling → contraction. Energy metabolism: ATP from creatine phosphate (immediate), glycolysis (short-term), oxidative phosphorylation (long-term). Fiber types: Type I (slow-twitch, oxidative, fatigue-resistant—postural muscles), Type IIa (fast-twitch, oxidative-glycolytic, intermediate fatigue), Type IIb/x (fast-twitch, glycolytic, fatigable—power muscles). Clinical relevance: Muscular dystrophy (Duchenne—dystrophin deficiency, progressive degeneration); myasthenia gravis (autoimmune antibodies against ACh receptors); atrophy (disuse, denervation); hypertrophy (resistance training).

examples.explanation: Skeletal muscle activation sequence: (1) Motor neuron releases acetylcholine (ACh) at neuromuscular junction; (2) ACh binds nicotinic receptors on sarcolemma → Na⁺ influx → end-plate potential; (3) Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open → action potential propagates along sarcolemma; (4) Depolarization travels down T-tubules (invaginations of sarcolemma); (5) Dihydropyridine receptors (DHPR) in T-tubules activate ryanodine receptors (RyR) in sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR); (6) Ca²⁺ released from SR into cytoplasm; (7) Ca²⁺ binds troponin C → conformational change moves tropomyosin away from myosin-binding sites on actin; (8) Myosin heads bind actin → cross-bridge cycling (ATP hydrolysis → power stroke → detachment); (9) Contraction continues while Ca²⁺ elevated; (10) Ca²⁺ pumped back into SR (SERCA) → relaxation. Clinical correlation: Botulinum toxin blocks ACh release → flaccid paralysis; Lambert-Eaton syndrome impairs ACh release; myasthenia gravis antibodies block ACh receptors.

image.alt: NMJ anatomy; action potential propagation; T-tubules; Ca²⁺ release; cross-bridge cycling
image.caption: Excitation-contraction coupling: ACh release → AP → T-tubule depolarization → RyR activation → Ca²⁺ release → troponin binding → cross-bridge cycling. Clinical disorders target each step.

examples.explanation: Fiber type classification and characteristics: (1) Type I (slow-twitch, oxidative): High mitochondrial density, high myoglobin (red), slow myosin ATPase, fatigue-resistant; recruited for posture, endurance activities; (2) Type IIa (fast-twitch, oxidative-glycolytic): Intermediate properties; fatigue moderately; recruited for sustained power; (3) Type IIb/x (fast-twitch, glycolytic): Low mitochondria, low myoglobin (white), fast myosin ATPase, fatigable; recruited for sprinting, jumping; (4) Fiber type distribution: Genetically determined but modifiable by training (endurance training converts IIb → IIa; resistance training increases fiber size, not type conversion); (5) Clinical: Muscle biopsy for diagnosis (metabolic myopathies, denervation atrophy). Significance: Fiber type specialization allows graded force production—recruitment order: Type I → IIa → IIb (Henneman's size principle).

image.alt: Type I, IIa, IIb fibers; myosin ATPase staining; mitochondrial content; fatigue resistance
image.caption: Skeletal muscle fiber types: Type I (slow, oxidative, fatigue-resistant—posture); Type IIa (fast, oxidative-glycolytic—sustained power); Type IIb (fast, glycolytic, fatigable—sprint). Recruitment order: I → IIa → IIb.

classifications.value: Best for understanding automaticity, rhythmic contraction, and myocardial function
characteristics: Location: Heart (myocardium)
Cell shape: Branched, cylindrical
Nuclei: Single, centrally located
Striation: Present (similar to skeletal but less organized)
Intercalated discs: Specialized cell-cell junctions—gap junctions (electrical coupling), desmosomes (mechanical adhesion)
Control: Involuntary (autonomic nervous system modulates; autorhythmic)
Contraction speed: Moderate (100-200 msec to peak tension)
Fatigue: Fatigue-resistant (contracts continuously throughout life)
Regeneration: Limited (minimal regenerative capacity; cardiomyocytes exit cell cycle postnatally)
classifications.behavior: Cardiac muscle is specialized for continuous, rhythmic contraction—pumping blood without fatigue for a lifetime. Key adaptations: Autorhythmicity—pacemaker cells (SA node, AV node) spontaneously depolarize (funny current, I_f), generating intrinsic rhythm (60-100 bpm). Intercalated discs: gap junctions (connexins) allow electrical coupling—action potentials spread directly from cell to cell, enabling synchronized contraction; desmosomes (cadherins) provide mechanical strength, preventing separation under tension. Excitation-contraction coupling: similar to skeletal but Ca²⁺ entry through L-type channels (dihydropyridine receptors) triggers additional Ca²⁺ release from SR (calcium-induced calcium release). Action potential: prolonged (200-300 msec) plateau phase (Ca²⁺ influx via L-type channels) prevents tetanus—ensures relaxation between beats, allowing ventricular filling. Refractory period: long (absolute refractory period nearly entire contraction) prevents premature beats. Metabolism: primarily oxidative (high mitochondria, 30-40% cell volume), fatigue-resistant. Regulation: autonomic nervous system (sympathetic—increases rate, contractility; parasympathetic—decreases rate); hormones (epinephrine, thyroid hormone). Clinical relevance: Myocardial infarction (ischemic death of cardiac muscle); arrhythmias (conduction disorders); heart failure (reduced contractility); cardiomyopathy (structural/functional myocardial disease).

examples.process: Cardiac Action Potential and Calcium-Induced Calcium Release
examples.explanation: Cardiac muscle electrophysiology: (1) Ventricular myocyte action potential phases: Phase 0—fast Na⁺ channels (rapid depolarization); Phase 1—transient K⁺ efflux (early repolarization); Phase 2—plateau (L-type Ca²⁺ channels open, Ca²⁺ influx balances K⁺ efflux); Phase 3—repolarization (K⁺ efflux); Phase 4—resting potential; (2) Calcium-induced calcium release (CICR): Ca²⁺ influx through L-type channels (DHPR) triggers RyR2 on SR to release additional Ca²⁺ (amplification); (3) Ca²⁺ binds troponin C → contraction; (4) Relaxation: Ca²⁺ pumped back into SR (SERCA) and extruded via NCX (Na⁺/Ca²⁺ exchanger); (5) Long refractory period prevents tetanus. Pacemaker cells (SA node): no stable resting potential; slow diastolic depolarization (funny current, I_f); autonomic modulation (sympathetic ↑ slope, parasympathetic ↓ slope). Clinical: Antiarrhythmics target ion channels (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, K⁺ channels); digoxin inhibits Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase, increases Ca²⁺, enhances contractility.

image.alt: Ventricular action potential phases; calcium-induced calcium release; pacemaker potential
image.caption: Cardiac muscle: Prolonged action potential (plateau) prevents tetanus. Calcium-induced calcium release (CICR): Ca²⁺ influx triggers SR Ca²⁺ release. Pacemaker cells spontaneously depolarize (I_f).

examples.process: Intercalated Discs: Gap Junctions and Desmosomes
examples.explanation: Intercalated disc structure and function: (1) Gap junctions (connexin 43): transmembrane channels allowing ions and small molecules to pass between adjacent cardiomyocytes; provide electrical coupling—action potentials spread rapidly (0.3-0.5 m/sec), enabling synchronized contraction; (2) Desmosomes (cadherin-based): mechanical junctions anchoring intermediate filaments (desmin); provide tensile strength, prevent separation under mechanical stress; (3) Fascia adherens: actin-binding junctions; (4) Clinical: Cardiomyopathies associated with desmosome mutations (arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy—ARVC); connexin mutations cause conduction disorders. Significance: Intercalated discs transform individual cardiomyocytes into functional syncytium—atria contract as unit, ventricles contract as unit. Comparison: Skeletal muscle fibers fuse into syncytium; cardiac muscle remains separate cells but functionally coupled via gap junctions.

image.alt: Intercalated disc structure: gap junctions (connexins), desmosomes (cadherins); electrical/mechanical coupling
image.caption: Intercalated discs: Gap junctions (electrical coupling—synchronized contraction) + Desmosomes (mechanical adhesion—tensile strength). Cardiomyocytes form functional syncytium; mutations cause cardiomyopathy, conduction disorders.

classifications.value: Best for understanding visceral function, tone maintenance, and pharmacologic regulation
characteristics: Location: Walls of hollow organs (blood vessels, GI tract, uterus, bladder, airways, ureters)
Cell shape: Spindle-shaped, tapered ends
Nuclei: Single, centrally located
Striation: Absent (no sarcomeres)
Contractile organization: Actin and myosin arranged diagonally, attached to dense bodies (analogous to Z-discs)
Control: Involuntary (autonomic nervous system, hormones, local factors, stretch)
Contraction speed: Slow (500-1000 msec to peak tension)
Fatigue: Fatigue-resistant (sustained tone with low energy cost)
Regeneration: Moderate regenerative capacity (hyperplasia in response to demand)
classifications.behavior: Smooth muscle is specialized for slow, sustained, energy-efficient contraction—maintaining tone, propelling contents through hollow organs, regulating flow. Classification: (1) Visceral (single-unit) smooth muscle—most common (GI tract, uterus, bladder); cells electrically coupled via gap junctions; contract as syncytium; spontaneous activity (slow waves, pacemaker activity from interstitial cells of Cajal). (2) Multi-unit smooth muscle—iris, ciliary body, vas deferens; cells independent, each innervated; fine control. Contractile mechanism: No sarcomeres; actin and myosin filaments arranged diagonally, attached to dense bodies (α-actinin, analogous to Z-discs) in cytoplasm and on sarcolemma. Excitation-contraction coupling: Ca²⁺ influx (L-type channels) or release from SR (IP₃ pathway) → Ca²⁺ binds calmodulin → activates myosin light chain kinase (MLCK) → phosphorylates myosin light chain (MLC) → cross-bridge cycling. Relaxation: myosin light chain phosphatase (MLCP) dephosphorylates MLC; Ca²⁺ removed (NCX, Ca²⁺ ATPase). Regulation: Autonomic nerves (sympathetic—usually relaxation in vascular, contraction in some; parasympathetic—contraction in GI, bladder); hormones (epinephrine, angiotensin, oxytocin); local factors (NO—vasodilation, histamine—contraction); stretch (myogenic response—vascular smooth muscle contracts when stretched). Latch state: sustained contraction with low ATP consumption (slow cross-bridge cycling, dephosphorylated myosin remains attached). Clinical relevance: Asthma (bronchospasm), hypertension (increased vascular tone), achalasia (esophageal dysmotility), overactive bladder, preterm labor (uterine smooth muscle).

examples.process: Smooth Muscle Excitation-Contraction Coupling and Regulation
examples.explanation: Smooth muscle activation pathways: (1) Electromechanical coupling: Depolarization (via autonomic nerves, stretch) opens voltage-gated L-type Ca²⁺ channels → Ca²⁺ influx → MLCK activation → contraction; (2) Pharmacomechanical coupling: Receptor activation (Gq-coupled: α₁-adrenergic, angiotensin II, endothelin) → PLC → IP₃ → SR Ca²⁺ release; (3) Ca²⁺-calmodulin complex activates MLCK; (4) MLC phosphorylates myosin light chain → cross-bridge cycling; (5) Relaxation: MLCP dephosphorylates MLC; Ca²⁺ removal (NCX, PMCA, SERCA); (6) Nitric oxide (NO) pathway: NO activates guanylyl cyclase → cGMP → PKG → MLCP activation, Ca²⁺ sequestration → relaxation (vasodilation). Clinical: Calcium channel blockers (nifedipine) relax vascular smooth muscle (hypertension), uterine smooth muscle (tocolysis); β₂-agonists (albuterol) relax bronchial smooth muscle (asthma); phosphodiesterase inhibitors (sildenafil) potentiate NO pathway (erectile dysfunction, pulmonary hypertension).

image.alt: Smooth muscle Ca²⁺ pathways: L-type channels, IP₃ receptors; MLCK/MLCP regulation; NO pathway
image.caption: Smooth muscle contraction: Ca²⁺ influx (L-type) or SR release (IP₃) → Ca²⁺-calmodulin → MLCK → MLC phosphorylation → cross-bridge cycling. Relaxation: MLCP dephosphorylation; NO-cGMP pathway enhances relaxation.

examples.explanation: Smooth muscle subtypes: (1) Single-unit (visceral): Cells electrically coupled via gap junctions; spontaneous electrical activity (slow waves, pacemaker); contract as syncytium; found in GI tract (interstitial cells of Cajal generate slow waves), uterus, bladder, ureters; responds to stretch (myogenic response); (2) Multi-unit: Each cell independently innervated; no gap junctions; fine, graded control; found in iris (pupil dilation/constriction), ciliary body (lens accommodation), vas deferens; (3) Clinical: GI motility disorders (slow wave abnormalities); overactive bladder (increased single-unit activity); achalasia (loss of inhibitory neurons in esophageal smooth muscle). Significance: Subtype specialization reflects functional demands—single-unit for coordinated propulsion (peristalsis), multi-unit for precise control (iris, ciliary body).

image.alt: Single-unit smooth muscle (gap junctions, syncytium) vs. multi-unit (independent innervation)
image.caption: Smooth muscle subtypes: Single-unit (visceral)—electrically coupled, syncytial contraction (GI tract, uterus). Multi-unit—independent cells, fine control (iris, ciliary body, vas deferens).

principles.principle: Sliding Filament Mechanism is Universal to All Muscle Types
principles.statement: All three muscle types contract via the sliding filament mechanism—myosin cross-bridges bind to actin, undergo power stroke, and generate force—though organization differs (sarcomeres in striated muscle; diagonal filaments with dense bodies in smooth muscle).
principles.application: Physiological inference: (1) Actin and myosin are conserved across muscle types; (2) Ca²⁺ is the universal trigger (binds troponin in striated; binds calmodulin in smooth); (3) ATP hydrolysis provides energy for cross-bridge cycling; (4) Force generation proportional to cross-bridge number. Clinical application: Understanding universal mechanism explains drug effects: calcium channel blockers affect all muscle types; ATP depletion causes rigor (postmortem). Evolutionary insight: Sliding filament mechanism evolved early in metazoans; muscle type diversification represents modifications of this core mechanism.
principles.example: Cross-bridge cycle: (1) Myosin head (ADP + Pi bound) binds actin; (2) Pi release → power stroke (myosin head pivots, sliding filament); (3) ADP released; (4) ATP binds myosin → detachment; (5) ATP hydrolysis → myosin head re-energized (cocks). In skeletal muscle, Ca²⁺ regulates via troponin; in smooth muscle, Ca²⁺-calmodulin activates MLCK, phosphorylating myosin light chain to permit cycling. All three types require ATP for both contraction (power stroke) and relaxation (Ca²⁺ pumping).
principles.analogy: Sliding filament mechanism is like rowing a boat: Myosin heads are oars; actin is water; Ca²⁺ is the coxswain giving signal. In skeletal muscle, coxswain (Ca²⁺) removes oar locks (tropomyosin); in smooth muscle, coxswain activates rowers (MLCK). All need oars (myosin), water (actin), and energy (ATP).

image.alt: Cross-bridge cycle stages; actin-myosin interaction; Ca²⁺ regulation in striated vs. smooth muscle
image.caption: Universal sliding filament mechanism: Myosin binds actin → power stroke → ATP binding → detachment. Ca²⁺ trigger differs: striated (troponin), smooth (calmodulin-MLCK).

principles.principle: Excitation-Contraction Coupling Links Electrical Signals to Contraction
principles.statement: All muscle types convert electrical depolarization into mechanical contraction via Ca²⁺—skeletal muscle uses T-tubule-coupled RyR activation; cardiac muscle uses calcium-induced calcium release; smooth muscle uses Ca²⁺ influx and IP₃-mediated SR release—reflecting structural specializations.
principles.application: Physiological inference: (1) Skeletal: T-tubules closely apposed to SR (triads); DHPR mechanically coupled to RyR1; rapid Ca²⁺ release; (2) Cardiac: T-tubules present; DHPR (L-type) opens → Ca²⁺ influx triggers RyR2 (CICR); (3) Smooth: Sparse T-tubules; Ca²⁺ entry via L-type channels; IP₃ receptors on SR; (4) Relaxation: all require Ca²⁺ removal (SERCA, NCX, PMCA). Clinical application: Malignant hyperthermia (RyR1 mutation—uncontrolled Ca²⁺ release); heart failure (impaired CICR); vasospasm (excessive smooth muscle Ca²⁺).
principles.example: EC coupling differences: (1) Skeletal: AP → T-tubule depolarization → DHPR conformational change opens RyR1 (mechanical coupling) → Ca²⁺ release; (2) Cardiac: AP → Ca²⁺ influx via DHPR → Ca²⁺ binds RyR2 → additional Ca²⁺ release; (3) Smooth: AP or receptor activation → Ca²⁺ influx or IP₃ → Ca²⁺ binds calmodulin → MLCK activation. Comparison: Skeletal fastest (msec), cardiac moderate, smooth slowest (sec).
principles.analogy: EC coupling is like dominoes: Skeletal = direct push (mechanical coupling); Cardiac = first domino falls, triggers second row (calcium-induced); Smooth = multiple triggers can start the chain (voltage, chemical, stretch). All end with Ca²⁺ 'dominoes' falling, leading to contraction.

image.alt: Skeletal (triad, DHPR-RyR coupling), cardiac (CICR), smooth (L-type, IP₃) EC coupling pathways
image.caption: Excitation-contraction coupling: Skeletal (mechanical DHPR-RyR coupling), Cardiac (calcium-induced calcium release), Smooth (L-type channels, IP₃ receptors). All increase cytoplasmic Ca²⁺ for contraction.

principles.principle: Muscle Type Specialization Reflects Functional Demands
principles.statement: Skeletal muscle optimizes speed and power (sacrificing fatigue resistance); cardiac muscle optimizes continuous rhythmic contraction (sacrificing regenerative capacity); smooth muscle optimizes sustained tone with low energy cost (sacrificing speed)—each specialized for its functional role.
principles.application: Evolutionary inference: (1) Skeletal: rapid recruitment, graded force via motor unit recruitment; fatigable but powerful; (2) Cardiac: autorhythmic, long refractory period prevents tetanus, fatigue-resistant; (3) Smooth: slow, sustained, latch state conserves ATP, maintains tone. Clinical application: Muscle-specific disorders reflect vulnerabilities—skeletal (muscular dystrophy, denervation), cardiac (ischemia, arrhythmia), smooth (asthma, hypertension).
principles.example: Functional comparison: (1) Skeletal: fastest contraction (30 msec), highest force, fatigable—enables sprinting (type IIb) and sustained posture (type I); (2) Cardiac: moderate speed (100-200 msec), moderate force, fatigue-resistant—beats 2.5 billion times/lifetime; (3) Smooth: slowest (500-1000 msec), lowest force, fatigue-resistant—maintains vascular tone continuously without fatigue. Energy efficiency: smooth muscle uses 10-100× less ATP per unit force than skeletal due to latch state.
principles.analogy: Muscle specialization is like engine types: Skeletal = race car engine (high power, high fuel consumption, requires rest); Cardiac = aircraft engine (runs continuously, moderate power, reliable); Smooth = diesel engine (slow, efficient, runs all day at low RPM). Each optimized for different tasks.

image.alt: Comparison graphs: contraction speed, fatigue resistance, force generation, energy efficiency
image.caption: Functional specialization: Skeletal (fast, high force, fatigable); Cardiac (moderate, fatigue-resistant, rhythmic); Smooth (slow, fatigue-resistant, energy-efficient, sustained tone).

components.definition: Muscle tissue components include contractile proteins (actin, myosin), regulatory proteins (troponin/tropomyosin in striated; calmodulin/MLCK in smooth), cytoskeletal elements (desmin, dystrophin), membrane systems (sarcolemma, T-tubules, sarcoplasmic reticulum), and connective tissue layers (epimysium, perimysium, endomysium in skeletal).
components.interpretation: Muscle force generation depends on the number of active cross-bridges (regulated by Ca²⁺), cycling rate (myosin ATPase isoform), and sarcomere length (optimal overlap in striated muscle). Different muscle types optimize these parameters for their functional demands.

image.alt: Skeletal muscle: epimysium, perimysium, endomysium, fiber, myofibril, sarcomere; cardiac: intercalated discs; smooth: dense bodies
image.caption: Muscle tissue components: Contractile (actin, myosin), regulatory (troponin/calmodulin), membrane systems (T-tubules, SR), cytoskeleton (dystrophin, desmin). Organization differs by muscle type.

categories.description: Skeletal muscle organized hierarchically: epimysium (surrounds entire muscle), perimysium (surrounds fascicles), endomysium (surrounds individual fibers)—provide structural support, transmit force, and contain blood vessels, nerves.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Connective tissue layers: (1) Epimysium: Dense irregular CT surrounding entire muscle; continuous with deep fascia; (2) Perimysium: Surrounds fascicles (bundles of fibers); contains larger blood vessels, nerves; (3) Endomysium: Reticular fibers, basal lamina surrounding each muscle fiber; contains capillaries, satellite cells; (4) Force transmission: All three layers converge to form tendons (at ends) and aponeuroses (broad sheets); transmit muscle force to bone; (5) Clinical: Myositis (inflammation of CT); compartment syndrome (pressure within epimysium compromises circulation); muscular dystrophy (dystrophin deficiency disrupts endomysium-sarcolemma connection).

examples.explanation: Dystrophin protein function: (1) Location: Sub-sarcolemmal cytoskeletal protein; connects intracellular actin cytoskeleton to extracellular matrix via dystrophin-glycoprotein complex (DGC); (2) Function: Provides mechanical reinforcement; stabilizes sarcolemma during contraction; (3) Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD): Dystrophin gene mutation (X-linked, frame-shift, absent dystrophin); progressive muscle degeneration; onset <5 years, wheelchair by 12, death by 20-30 (respiratory/cardiac failure); (4) Becker muscular dystrophy (BMD): Partial dystrophin deficiency; milder, later onset; (5) Pathophysiology: Without dystrophin, sarcolemma tears during contraction → Ca²⁺ influx → necrosis, inflammation, fibrosis. Significance: Dystrophin essential for skeletal muscle integrity; deficiency causes severe myopathy. This demonstrates muscle-specific structural protein function.

image.alt: Dystrophin-glycoprotein complex; Duchenne vs. Becker; sarcolemma instability; muscle histology
image.caption: Dystrophin: Connects actin cytoskeleton to ECM. Duchenne MD (absent dystrophin) → sarcolemma tears during contraction → Ca²⁺ influx → necrosis, fibrosis, progressive weakness.

categories.description: Specialized cell-cell junctions enabling electrical and mechanical coupling: gap junctions (connexin 43, electrical coupling), desmosomes (cadherin-based, mechanical adhesion), fascia adherens (actin anchoring).
categories.detailed_mechanism: Intercalated disc components: (1) Gap junctions (connexin 43): Hexameric channels (connexons) allow ions, small molecules to pass; provide electrical coupling—action potentials propagate directly between cells (velocity 0.3-0.5 m/sec); (2) Desmosomes (desmoglein, desmocollin): Cadherin family; anchor intermediate filaments (desmin); resist mechanical stress; (3) Fascia adherens: N-cadherin-based; anchor actin filaments; (4) Clinical: Arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (ARVC)—desmosome mutations (plakoglobin, desmoplakin); fibrofatty replacement; arrhythmias, sudden death. Significance: Intercalated discs transform individual cardiomyocytes into functional syncytium—essential for coordinated pumping.

examples.explanation: ARVC pathophysiology: (1) Genetics: Autosomal dominant; desmosome protein mutations (plakoglobin, desmoplakin, plakophilin-2); (2) Mechanism: Impaired desmosome function → reduced mechanical coupling → myocyte separation, apoptosis; (3) Pathologic replacement: Fibrofatty infiltration of myocardium (especially RV); (4) Clinical: Ventricular arrhythmias (tachycardia, fibrillation), sudden death (often in young athletes); (5) Diagnosis: ECG (epsilon wave), MRI (fat infiltration), genetic testing. Significance: Demonstrates critical role of intercalated discs in cardiac mechanical and electrical integrity; desmosome mutations cause cardiomyopathy and arrhythmias.

image.alt: Desmosome structure; ARVC pathology (fibrofatty replacement); ECG epsilon wave; MRI
image.caption: ARVC: Desmosome mutations → reduced mechanical coupling → myocyte apoptosis → fibrofatty replacement → arrhythmias, sudden death. Demonstrates intercalated disc critical function.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Muscle Type Analysis in Clinical Context

worked_examples.title: Differentiating Muscle Type Disorders
worked_examples.problem: A 45-year-old male presents with progressive weakness (difficulty rising from chair, climbing stairs), ptosis (eyelid drooping) worse with fatigue. A 60-year-old female presents with chest pain radiating to left arm, ECG shows ST elevation. A 30-year-old female presents with episodic wheezing, chest tightness. Using muscle tissue principles: (1) Which muscle types are affected in each case? (2) What cellular mechanisms explain each presentation? (3) How do treatment approaches differ based on muscle type?

step_by_step.explanation: Identify affected muscle types based on presentation.
step_by_step.calculation: Case 1 (progressive weakness, ptosis, fatigable): Skeletal muscle disorder (myasthenia gravis—autoimmune ACh receptor antibodies). Case 2 (chest pain, ST elevation): Cardiac muscle disorder (myocardial infarction—ischemic necrosis). Case 3 (wheezing, chest tightness): Smooth muscle disorder (asthma—bronchospasm).

Myasthenia gravis: Autoantibodies against nicotinic ACh receptors at neuromuscular junction; reduced receptor number → decreased end-plate potential → fatigable weakness (worse with repeated use). Myocardial infarction: Coronary artery occlusion → ischemia → ATP depletion → failure of Ca²⁺ pumps, Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase → cell swelling, necrosis → loss of contractile function. Asthma: Trigger (allergen, exercise) → mast cell degranulation → histamine, leukotrienes → smooth muscle contraction (MLCK activation) → bronchoconstriction, airway narrowing.

step_by_step.explanation: Compare treatment approaches based on muscle type physiology.
step_by_step.calculation: Myasthenia gravis: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (pyridostigmine) increase ACh at NMJ; immunosuppression (prednisone, rituximab); thymectomy (thymoma associated). Myocardial infarction: Reperfusion (PCI, thrombolysis) to restore blood flow; antiplatelets, anticoagulants; β-blockers reduce myocardial O₂ demand; ACE inhibitors prevent remodeling. Asthma: β₂-agonists (albuterol) relax bronchial smooth muscle via cAMP → PKA → MLCK inhibition, MLCP activation; corticosteroids reduce inflammation; leukotriene modifiers (montelukast) block bronchoconstrictor mediators.

step_by_step.explanation: Summarize muscle type-specific pathophysiology and treatment.
step_by_step.calculation: Skeletal muscle disorders target NMJ (myasthenia), contractile proteins (muscular dystrophy), or innervation (ALS). Cardiac muscle disorders involve ischemia (MI), conduction (arrhythmias), or contractility (heart failure). Smooth muscle disorders involve tone (asthma, hypertension), motility (achalasia, IBS), or sphincter function (GERD). Treatment targets muscle-specific physiology: skeletal (ACh modulation, immunosuppression), cardiac (hemodynamics, reperfusion), smooth (receptor-specific agonists/antagonists).

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Case 1: Skeletal muscle (myasthenia gravis); Case 2: Cardiac muscle (myocardial infarction); Case 3: Smooth muscle (asthma). (2) Mechanisms: Myasthenia—autoimmune ACh receptor blockade; MI—ischemic necrosis; Asthma—bronchial smooth muscle contraction via MLCK activation. (3) Treatments target muscle-specific physiology: pyridostigmine (skeletal ACh), reperfusion/β-blockers (cardiac), β₂-agonists (smooth muscle relaxation).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Muscle tissue types differ in structure, regulation, and pathology. Skeletal muscle (voluntary, NMJ-dependent) affected in myasthenia; cardiac muscle (autorhythmic, oxygen-dependent) affected in ischemia; smooth muscle (autonomic-regulated) affected in asthma. Understanding muscle type physiology guides diagnosis and targeted therapy.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: Muscle Contraction and Relaxation
sections.introduction: Muscle contraction involves a sequence of events from neural activation (or intrinsic rhythm) to force generation and subsequent relaxation. Understanding this flow reveals commonalities and differences across muscle types.

process_flow.description: Muscle receives stimulus: neural (skeletal, smooth) or intrinsic (cardiac pacemaker).
process_flow.mechanism: Skeletal: Motor neuron releases ACh → end-plate potential → AP. Cardiac: SA node pacemaker → spontaneous depolarization → AP spreads via gap junctions. Smooth: Autonomic nerves, hormones, stretch → depolarization or receptor activation.
process_flow.result: Membrane depolarization; voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels (cardiac, smooth) or T-tubule depolarization (skeletal) trigger Ca²⁺ increase

image.alt: Skeletal: NMJ; Cardiac: SA node; Smooth: autonomic varicosities; depolarization pathways
image.caption: Step 1: Activation—Skeletal (NMJ, ACh), Cardiac (SA node pacemaker, gap junctions), Smooth (autonomic nerves, hormones, stretch). All lead to membrane depolarization, Ca²⁺ increase.

process_flow.description: Ca²⁺ increase triggers contractile machinery activation.
process_flow.mechanism: Skeletal: T-tubule depolarization → DHPR-RyR1 coupling → SR Ca²⁺ release. Cardiac: L-type Ca²⁺ influx → RyR2 activation (CICR) → additional SR release. Smooth: Ca²⁺ influx (L-type) + IP₃-mediated SR release → Ca²⁺ binds calmodulin → MLCK activation → myosin phosphorylation.

image.alt: Skeletal (triad), cardiac (CICR), smooth (MLCK) EC coupling pathways
image.caption: Step 2: EC coupling—Skeletal (mechanical RyR activation), Cardiac (calcium-induced), Smooth (calmodulin-MLCK). All increase cytoplasmic Ca²⁺ to activate cross-bridge cycling.

process_flow.description: Myosin heads bind actin, undergo power stroke, generating force.
process_flow.mechanism: Myosin-ADP-Pi binds actin → Pi release → power stroke (myosin pivots) → ADP release → ATP binding → detachment → ATP hydrolysis → myosin re-energized. Cycles continue while Ca²⁺ elevated. Force depends on cross-bridge number, cycling rate, sarcomere length (striated) or dense body organization (smooth).
process_flow.result: Force generated; sarcomere shortening (striated) or cell contraction (smooth)

image.alt: Cross-bridge cycle stages; ATP binding; power stroke; force generation
image.caption: Step 3: Cross-bridge cycling—Myosin binds actin → power stroke (force) → ATP binding → detachment → re-energizing. Continues while Ca²⁺ elevated and ATP available.

process_flow.description: Ca²⁺ removed from cytoplasm; cross-bridge cycling ceases; muscle returns to resting length.
process_flow.mechanism: Ca²⁺ pumped back into SR (SERCA) and/or extruded from cell (NCX, PMCA). In striated muscle, Ca²⁺ removal allows troponin to revert → tropomyosin blocks actin-binding sites. In smooth, MLCP dephosphorylates myosin light chain → cross-bridge detachment (latch state may persist with dephosphorylated attached myosin).
process_flow.result: Muscle relaxation; returns to resting state; ready for next activation

image.alt: SERCA, NCX, PMCA; troponin-tropomyosin; MLCP; latch state
image.caption: Step 4: Relaxation—Ca²⁺ removed (SERCA, NCX). Striated: troponin-tropomyosin blocks actin. Smooth: MLCP dephosphorylates myosin; latch state maintains tone with low ATP use.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Muscle Development and Regeneration
sections.definition: Muscle tissue development involves myoblast differentiation, fusion (skeletal, cardiac), and maturation; regenerative capacity varies: skeletal muscle has satellite cells (limited), cardiac muscle minimal, smooth muscle moderate hyperplasia.

process_types.energy_profile: Myoblasts proliferate, differentiate, fuse to form multinucleated myotubes; satellite cells mediate postnatal growth and regeneration
process_types.direction: Mesodermal precursor → Myoblast proliferation → Myoblast fusion → Myotube formation → Myofiber maturation → Satellite cell quiescence → Injury activation → Proliferation, differentiation → Fusion, regeneration
process_types.function: Form skeletal muscle during development; maintain and repair muscle throughout life
goals: Understand myogenesis (MyoD, myogenin, Myf5, MRF4 regulation)
Explain satellite cell function in regeneration
Identify causes of muscle atrophy, hypertrophy
process_types.energy_source: Developmental biology, stem cell research, molecular genetics

primary_example.explanation: Satellite cell function: (1) Location: Between basal lamina and sarcolemma; quiescent in adult muscle; (2) Activation: Injury (trauma, exercise) → release of growth factors (HGF, FGF) → satellite cell activation, proliferation; (3) Differentiation: Myogenic regulatory factors (MyoD, myogenin) drive myoblast differentiation; (4) Fusion: Myoblasts fuse to each other or existing fibers, repairing damage; (5) Clinical: Muscular dystrophy (repeated cycles of degeneration/regeneration exhaust satellite cells → fibrosis, fat infiltration); sarcopenia (age-related satellite cell decline, impaired regeneration). Significance: Satellite cells provide limited regenerative capacity; exhaustion leads to fibrosis. Therapeutic: stem cell therapy, gene editing (CRISPR for DMD).

image.alt: Satellite cell location; activation, proliferation, differentiation; DMD pathology
image.caption: Satellite cells: Quiescent stem cells between basal lamina and sarcolemma. Injury → activation → proliferation → differentiation → fusion → regeneration. Exhaustion in Duchenne MD leads to fibrosis.

image.alt: Myoblast fusion, myotube formation, myofiber maturation; MyoD, myogenin regulation
image.caption: Skeletal muscle development: Myoblasts (MyoD, Myf5) → fusion → myotubes → myofibers. Satellite cells maintain postnatal regenerative capacity. Regeneration declines with age (sarcopenia).

process_types.energy_profile: Cardiomyocytes differentiate from mesoderm; proliferate during fetal/neonatal period; exit cell cycle postnatally; minimal regenerative capacity
process_types.direction: Mesodermal precursor → Cardiac progenitor → Cardiomyocyte differentiation → Proliferation (fetal/neonatal) → Cell cycle exit → Hypertrophic growth (postnatal) → Minimal regeneration after injury
process_types.function: Form functional myocardium; maintain contractile function throughout life
process_types.energy_source: Developmental biology, stem cell research, regenerative medicine

primary_example.explanation: Cardiac regenerative limitations: (1) Neonatal heart: Limited regenerative capacity (first week in mice, minimal in humans); (2) Adult heart: Cardiomyocytes exit cell cycle postnatally; mitosis extremely rare (<0.01% annually); (3) Myocardial infarction: Necrotic tissue replaced by scar (fibroblasts, collagen) → loss of contractile function; (4) Therapeutic approaches: Stem cell transplantation (limited success); reprogramming fibroblasts to cardiomyocytes; stimulating endogenous cardiomyocyte proliferation (cell cycle regulators); (5) Differences from skeletal: No satellite cell equivalent; cardiac fibroblasts, not myocytes, proliferate after injury. Significance: Limited cardiac regeneration underlies heart failure post-MI; understanding developmental cell cycle exit may enable regenerative therapies.

image.alt: Neonatal vs. adult cardiomyocyte proliferation; MI scar formation; stem cell therapies
image.caption: Cardiac muscle: Minimal regenerative capacity; cardiomyocytes exit cell cycle postnatally. MI → scar (fibrosis) → loss of function. Therapeutic approaches: stem cells, cardiomyocyte proliferation induction.

image.alt: Cardiac progenitor differentiation; fetal proliferation; postnatal hypertrophy; heart development
image.caption: Cardiac development: Mesoderm → cardiac progenitors → cardiomyocyte differentiation. Proliferation peaks perinatally, declines postnatally. Adult cardiomyocytes undergo hypertrophy, not hyperplasia.

sections.relationship: Muscle development shares common myogenic regulatory factors (MyoD family) across types, but regenerative capacity varies: skeletal (satellite cells, limited), cardiac (minimal), smooth (moderate hyperplasia). Understanding developmental and regenerative biology informs therapies for muscle disorders—stem cell transplantation, gene therapy, tissue engineering.

sections.purpose: Myogenesis is regulated by myogenic regulatory factors (MRFs: MyoD, Myf5, myogenin, MRF4) and their upstream regulators (Pax3, Pax7), with muscle type-specific transcription factors (Nkx2.5 for cardiac, myocardin for smooth).

activation.definition: Myogenic regulatory factors (MRFs) are basic helix-loop-helix transcription factors that activate muscle-specific gene expression (actin, myosin, dystrophin, etc.). MyoD and Myf5 determine myoblast identity; myogenin and MRF4 drive differentiation. Cardiac muscle requires Nkx2.5, GATA4; smooth muscle requires myocardin, SRF.
activation.effect: MyoD overexpression converts fibroblasts to skeletal muscle cells (demonstrating master regulator role). Pax3/Pax7 regulate MRF expression in muscle precursors. In cardiac muscle, Nkx2.5 and GATA4 coordinate cardiomyocyte differentiation; mutations cause congenital heart defects. Smooth muscle differentiation: myocardin co-activates SRF (serum response factor) to activate smooth muscle-specific genes (α-actin, calponin). Understanding these pathways enables regenerative medicine approaches (direct reprogramming of fibroblasts to myocytes).

image.alt: MRF hierarchy: Pax3/7 → MyoD/Myf5 → myogenin/MRF4; cardiac (Nkx2.5, GATA4); smooth (myocardin, SRF)
image.caption: Myogenic regulation: Pax3/Pax7 → MyoD/Myf5 (determination) → myogenin/MRF4 (differentiation). Cardiac: Nkx2.5, GATA4. Smooth: myocardin-SRF. MRFs activate muscle-specific genes.

key_properties.property: MyoD: Master Skeletal Muscle Regulatory Factor
key_properties.explanation: MyoD function: (1) Basic helix-loop-helix transcription factor; (2) Activates muscle-specific genes (myosin heavy chain, actin, dystrophin, creatine kinase); (3) MyoD knockout mice have normal muscle (compensated by Myf5); MyoD/Myf5 double knockout lacks skeletal muscle; (4) Ectopic expression: MyoD converts fibroblasts to myoblasts (demonstrates master regulator); (5) Clinical: Rhabdomyosarcoma (pediatric muscle cancer) often has MyoD expression; used diagnostically. Significance: MyoD demonstrates transcription factor hierarchy in cell fate determination. Therapeutic potential: MyoD delivery to damaged muscle may enhance regeneration; reprogramming fibroblasts to myogenic cells for tissue engineering.

image.alt: MyoD structure; MyoD/Myf5 knockout; fibroblast conversion; rhabdomyosarcoma
image.caption: MyoD: Master skeletal muscle regulator. MyoD/Myf5 double knockout → no skeletal muscle. Ectopic MyoD converts fibroblasts to myoblasts. Therapeutic potential for muscle regeneration.

key_properties.explanation: Nkx2.5 function: (1) Homeobox transcription factor; essential for cardiac development; (2) Regulates genes: atrial natriuretic factor (ANF), connexin 40, cardiac actin; (3) Knockout: Nkx2.5 null mice have arrested heart development (cardiac looping fails); (4) Human mutations: Nkx2.5 mutations cause congenital heart defects (atrial septal defect, tetralogy of Fallot, conduction abnormalities); (5) Acts with GATA4, TBX5 to coordinate cardiomyocyte differentiation. Significance: Nkx2.5 exemplifies muscle type-specific transcription factors; mutations cause common congenital heart defects. Understanding cardiac-specific regulation enables directed differentiation of stem cells to cardiomyocytes for regenerative therapy.

image.alt: Nkx2.5 expression; knockout heart phenotype; human congenital heart defects; GATA4, TBX5
image.caption: Nkx2.5: Cardiac transcription factor; essential for heart development. Knockout → arrested cardiac looping. Human mutations cause congenital heart defects (ASD, Tetralogy of Fallot).

key_properties.explanation: Myocardin function: (1) Serum response factor (SRF) co-activator; (2) Activates smooth muscle-specific genes (α-actin, calponin, SM22α); (3) Expressed in cardiac and smooth muscle; (4) Knockout: Myocardin null mice die embryonically (no vascular smooth muscle); (5) Regulation: TGF-β, PDGF signaling modulate myocardin activity; (6) Clinical: Myocardin expression altered in vascular diseases (atherosclerosis, restenosis). Significance: Myocardin-SRF complex specifies smooth muscle differentiation; understanding this pathway enables vascular tissue engineering, therapeutic modulation for vascular diseases (hypertension, atherosclerosis).

image.alt: Myocardin-SRF complex; smooth muscle gene activation; knockout phenotype; vascular disease
image.caption: Myocardin: SRF co-activator; activates smooth muscle genes (α-actin, calponin). Myocardin knockout → no vascular smooth muscle, embryonic lethal. Therapeutic target for vascular diseases.

summary: Muscle tissue comprises three types with distinct structure, function, and regulation. Skeletal muscle: voluntary, striated, multinucleated fibers; attached to bones; rapid, powerful contractions; fatigable; innervated by somatic motor neurons via neuromuscular junctions (ACh). Cardiac muscle: involuntary, striated, branched cells with single central nuclei; intercalated discs (gap junctions for electrical coupling, desmosomes for mechanical strength); autorhythmic (pacemaker cells); fatigue-resistant; innervated by autonomic nervous system (modulates rate, contractility). Smooth muscle: involuntary, non-striated, spindle-shaped cells with single central nuclei; walls of hollow organs (blood vessels, GI tract, uterus, bladder); slow, sustained contractions; maintains tone with low energy cost (latch state); innervated by autonomic nerves, hormones, local factors, stretch. All three share sliding filament mechanism: myosin cross-bridges bind actin, undergo power stroke (ATP hydrolysis), generating force. Ca²⁺ is universal trigger: skeletal (troponin), cardiac (troponin), smooth (calmodulin-MLCK). Excitation-contraction coupling: skeletal (T-tubule DHPR mechanically coupled to RyR1), cardiac (calcium-induced calcium release—CICR), smooth (L-type Ca²⁺ channels, IP₃-mediated SR release). Energy metabolism: skeletal (creatine phosphate, glycolysis, oxidative), cardiac (primarily oxidative, fatigue-resistant), smooth (oxidative, energy-efficient). Fiber types: skeletal (Type I slow-twitch oxidative, Type IIa fast oxidative-glycolytic, Type IIb/x fast glycolytic). Clinical correlations: muscular dystrophy (dystrophin deficiency—Duchenne), myasthenia gravis (ACh receptor antibodies), myocardial infarction (ischemic necrosis), asthma (bronchial smooth muscle constriction), malignant hyperthermia (RyR1 mutation), ARVC (desmosome mutations), sarcopenia (age-related atrophy). Development: skeletal from mesoderm, myoblast fusion (MyoD, Myf5, myogenin); cardiac (Nkx2.5, GATA4); smooth (myocardin-SRF). Regeneration: skeletal (satellite cells, limited), cardiac (minimal), smooth (moderate hyperplasia). Understanding muscle tissue types is fundamental for physiology (movement, circulation, digestion), pharmacology (neuromuscular blockers, β-agonists, calcium channel blockers), pathology (muscle-specific diseases), and clinical medicine (diagnosis, treatment, rehabilitation).
